title: How the sensation of being cold activates memories of neglect
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
It starts with a chill you can't explain 

 I was sitting in my living room last winter. The heat was on. I had a blanket. My coffee was warm in my hands....
htmlContent:
It starts with a chill you can't explain

I was sitting in my living room last winter. The heat was on. I had a blanket. My coffee was warm in my hands. But I felt cold. Deep cold. The kind that lives in your bones and whispers that something is wrong. I checked the thermostat. Seventy degrees. I checked my body. Not shivering. But my chest felt hollow. My shoulders were tight. My mind kept drifting to a memory I couldn't quite grab. And then I realized: I wasn't cold from the air. I was cold from the past.

Here's the thing about trauma. Your body doesn't know time. It doesn't understand that you're a grown adult with a heating system and a down comforter. Your body knows patterns. It knows sensations. And when it feels a certain temperature drop, a certain draft, a certain damp chill on your skin, it doesn't ask questions. It just reacts. It goes straight to the file marked "neglect" and pulls up everything you've tried to forget.

I'm not making this up. Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades showing how the nervous system scans for cues of safety and danger without our conscious awareness. It's called neuroception. Your body is constantly reading the environment. Temperature is one of those cues. A sudden cold sensation can trigger your vagus nerve to shift into dorsal vagal shutdown, which is the freeze response. And freeze, for many of us, is the place where neglect lives.

Stay with me here. Think about what neglect actually feels like. It's not usually a single traumatic event. It's an absence. A lack of warmth. A room that's too quiet. A parent who doesn't come when you cry. A bed that stays cold because no one checks on you. The physical sensation of being cold becomes linked to the emotional experience of being alone. Your body learns: cold = no one is coming.

I remember sitting in my childhood bedroom. The radiator was barely warm. My window didn't seal right. I could feel the draft on my face while I tried to sleep. And I remember thinking, even as a kid, that this was just how life was. You're cold. You deal with it. No one brings you another blanket. No one tucks you in. You learn to stop asking. That's the part that sticks. Not just the cold, but the certainty that your discomfort doesn't matter to anyone else.

Why temperature triggers the oldest wounds

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," talks about how trauma is stored in the body as implicit memory. These are memories that don't have words. They don't have a timeline. They don't say "this happened when I was seven." They say "this is happening right now." So when you feel a chill, your body doesn't think "oh, it's cold outside, I should put on a sweater." Your body thinks "danger. abandonment. threat." And it responds accordingly.

For people with a history of emotional neglect, the cold sensation can activate something deeper than a shiver. It can activate the part of you that learned to disappear. The part that went quiet so you wouldn't be a burden. The part that stopped crying because no one came anyway. That's the dorsal vagal response. It's the oldest part of your nervous system. It's the one that says "play dead and maybe the threat will pass." And neglect, for a child, is a threat. It's a threat to survival. Because children literally need attuned caregivers to regulate their nervous systems. Without that warmth, the system learns to shut down.

Wild, right? That a simple temperature change can send you into a state that mimics collapse. But it's real. I've had clients tell me they can't tolerate being cold. They keep their homes at seventy-five degrees year round. They wear sweaters in summer. They panic when they feel a draft. And it's not about the cold itself. It's about what the cold means. It means no one is coming. It means you're on your own. It means you have to survive by making yourself small.

One thing that helps some people is supporting the nervous system with nutrients that calm the stress response. One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium glycinate is known for its ability to relax muscles and soothe the nervous system, which can be helpful when your body is stuck in a cold-induced freeze state. It's not a cure. But it can take the edge off.

I want to be honest with you. This isn't about fixing the cold sensation. It's about understanding it. When you know that your body is reacting to a stored memory, not a current threat, you can start to separate the two. You can feel the chill and say "that's the past." But that takes practice. And it takes patience. Because your body has been doing this for a long time. It's not going to stop overnight.

The difference between being cold and feeling neglected

Here's where it gets tricky. Not every cold sensation is a trauma trigger. Sometimes you're just cold. You need a jacket. You need to turn up the heat. That's normal. But for people with a history of neglect, the line between physical cold and emotional cold gets blurred. The body doesn't have a separate system for "temperature" and "attachment." They're wired together. They developed together. When you were an infant, warmth meant safety. Warmth meant someone was holding you. Warmth meant you were going to survive. Cold meant the opposite.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma is held in the body's incomplete responses. If you were cold as a child and no one came, your body never got to complete the action of seeking warmth. The impulse to reach out, to call for help, to be held, it got stuck. So now, when you feel cold, your body activates that old impulse. But there's nowhere for it to go. You can't go back in time and get your needs met. So the energy stays trapped. And you feel it as a vague sense of dread, or a hollow feeling in your chest, or a desire to curl up and disappear.

I've had moments where I'm perfectly warm physically but I feel frozen inside. I'll be lying in bed under a heavy duvet, and my hands will be cold, and my mind will start spinning with old stories. "You're alone. No one cares. You have to take care of yourself because no one else will." And I have to remind myself that those stories are not about right now. They're about then. But telling the difference takes a lot of self-awareness. It takes a lot of compassion for the part of you that's still waiting for someone to come.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helped me understand why I learned to associate coldness with neglect. It explains how emotionally immature parents fail to provide the warmth that children need, not just physically but emotionally. And that lack of warmth becomes a template for how you see the world. You start to expect coldness. You start to brace for it. And your nervous system stays on high alert, scanning for any sign that you're about to be abandoned again.

Think about it. How many times have you felt a sudden chill and immediately felt sad? Or anxious? Or hopeless? That's not a coincidence. That's your body's memory system at work. The cold is a key that unlocks a door you didn't even know was there. And behind that door is a younger version of you who is still waiting for someone to notice that they're cold.

What to do when the cold memory hits

First, don't try to make it go away. That's what we're taught, right? Feel bad, fix it. Take a hot shower. Drink tea. Put on layers. And those things can help. But if you're just bypassing the sensation, you're not actually processing it. You're just covering it up. And it will come back. It always comes back.

Instead, try this. When you feel that cold sensation and you notice the familiar dread or loneliness or numbness, pause. Take a breath. And say to yourself: "This is a memory. This is not the present moment." You don't have to believe it right away. You just have to say it. Then bring your attention to something in the present. The weight of your body on the chair. The sound of a clock ticking. The texture of your shirt. Ground yourself in the here and now. Because the cold wants to pull you into the past. Your job is to stay here.

You can also try warming yourself in a way that feels intentional, not reactive. Instead of just piling on blankets, actually touch your own arms. Rub your hands together. Put your hand on your chest and say "I'm here. I'm safe. I can take care of myself now." It might feel silly. It might feel like it's not working. But you're sending a signal to your nervous system that the pattern has changed. That you're not a helpless child anymore. That you have the ability to provide your own warmth.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk lays out the science in a way that makes you feel less crazy. He explains why your body reacts the way it does, and he offers practical approaches for healing. It's not an easy read, but it's a validating one. It helps you understand that your cold sensations are not weakness. They're your body's way of trying to protect you.

I also want to say this. If the cold triggers are severe, if they send you into a state of dissociation or panic that lasts for hours, please consider working with a therapist who understands trauma and the nervous system. Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, and Internal Family Systems are all approaches that can help you untangle these old patterns. You don't have to do this alone. In fact, you probably can't do it alone. We're wired for connection. That's why the cold hurts so much in the first place. Because connection is the warmth we were denied.

Healing Journey Resources

If you're looking for more support on this path, here are a few resources that I've found helpful and that many others have too.

  • Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link) - A supplement that can support nervous system calm.
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - A book for understanding how trauma creates different parts of the self.
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - A foundational book on trauma and the body.
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - A book that validates the experience of growing up without emotional warmth.
